title: 🔴LIVE - My AI Coding Workflow has 10x'd Again with Archon - See it in Action
summary: Cole Medin showcases how Archon has significantly improved his AI coding workflow with Claude. He demonstrates live coding practices and claims Archon can 10x output when used with Claude Code and other models.
writeup: Archon is an open-source harness builder for AI coding whose central argument is an inversion: instead of forcing a software development process into LLM prompts and skills (leaving the entire lifecycle non-deterministic), developers build LLMs into a structured workflow where only steps that need reasoning, such as writing code, invoke a model. The live stream demonstrates this philosophy on a real production codebase by working through GitHub issues (brownfield) and bootstrapping a workflow marketplace (greenfield) where contributors host self-contained bundles and install them via a single command.
key_points: Archon's defining distinction from prompt-heavy coding agents is architectural: the software development lifecycle is encoded as a graph of workflow nodes (planning, implementation, validation, PR creation/merge), with LLM calls reserved strictly for steps requiring reasoning while deterministic code handles the rest, making the overall process as repeatable and predictable as possible.

A workflow marketplace is the greenfield feature being built live: contributors host a self-contained bundle in their own repository (core YAML file, deterministic-node scripts, and any included skills), then submit a pull request to a central registry, enabling single-command install analogous to NPM.

Provider mixing is a first-class feature: one workflow can send the implementation step to Claude Code while routing validation to Codex or any other supported model, with explicit parameters controlling how context passes between nodes.
Work trees run in isolation under the hood, so concurrent or parallel agent invocations within Archon do not contaminate each other's state.
The stream uses Archon's own production codebase as the working example rather than a contrived demo, with open GitHub issues serving as the structured input that drives the brownfield coding agent.
